However, the Senate added an amendment to the latest version: prohibiting any platform from providing returns, directly or indirectly, for passively held stablecoins, including any arrangement that is economically or functionally equivalent to bank deposit interest. In short, your "earn interest on holding cryptocurrencies" scheme is no longer allowed. This ban hits Circle where it hurts. If returns can't be paid, no one will have the incentive to hold USDC in the short term, and in the long term, Coinbase's stablecoin business will shrink. Coinbase earned $364 million in stablecoin revenue in the fourth quarter, a sum now hanging in the balance. The power struggle behind the ban is more interesting than the ban itself. This battle over stablecoin yields has actually been going on for almost a year. The GENIUS Act, signed into effect in July 2025, prohibits issuers from paying interest directly to holders, but the law does not explicitly cover related parties and third-party platforms. Circle and Coinbase exploited this gray area: Circle shared its reserve interest with Coinbase, which then distributed it to users as "rewards," ensuring the money still reached their accounts after going through a loop. More than 40 banking associations, led by the American Bankers Association, subsequently sent a joint letter to Congress demanding that this loophole be closed. The yield ban in the latest draft of the Clarity Act is a direct product of this lobbying effort. The traditional banking industry in the United States is driving this amendment. The logic is simple: if Coinbase can offer a 3.5% yield, why would customers keep their money in traditional bank current accounts? This yield mechanism of stablecoins directly undermines the foundation of bank deposits. Reportedly, bank representatives were scheduled to review the draft text on the day it was released. The crypto industry wants to do what banks do; banks will never sit idly by and watch their deposits be siphoned away by a group of coders. Lawmakers on Capitol Hill need to choose between two forces, and at least judging from this draft bill, traditional forces have temporarily gained the upper hand. Shay Boloor, chief market strategist at Futurum Equities, points out a more crucial issue: this ban blocks USDC's path from a payment instrument to a "store of value." And that upgrade path was precisely the core logic behind Circle's soaring stock price in recent weeks. The 170% increase was based on the market's pricing in the future potential of USDC. Now that potential has been slashed by the draft bill, the 170% increase has become the best reason to short it. The stock price plummeted from a high of $125 to around $101, marking its biggest single-day drop since its June 2025 IPO. Coinbase, also dragged down, fell by about 10%. Tether's "taking advantage of your weakness" To make matters worse, on the same day, Circle's biggest competitor, Tether, announced it had hired a Big Four accounting firm to conduct a formal audit of all its USDT reserves. This news might normally be just gossip, but at this time, its impact is amplified tenfold. The stablecoin landscape has always been clearly defined: Tether's USDT has the largest scale, but its reserve transparency is heavily questioned; Circle's USDC is smaller, but its compliance is solid. Institutional investors choose USDC largely because they are buying "peace of mind." Circle's moat ultimately boils down to trust. Now Tether is addressing this weakness. Once the audit is passed, the biggest gap between USDT and USDC will be closed. Regulators are digging at Circle's moat, while competitors are building their own. This double whammy, coupled with the precise timing, is hard to call a coincidence. On the eve of a reshaping of the stablecoin regulatory landscape, Tether's decision to reveal its sincerity at this window is a shrewd move. Some market analysts bluntly state that if Tether obtains the backing of the Big Four audits and further expands its presence in the US market, USDC's market share among institutional investors will be further eroded. Circle's carefully cultivated image as a "compliance champion" over the past few years is turning from a unique advantage into an entry barrier. Armor and Shackles. However, there are also sobering voices. Clear Street analyst Owen Lau believes the market reaction is excessive. He says the actual situation isn't as bad as the headlines suggest, and is more like a knee-jerk reaction to complex legislative news. He has a point; the CLARITY Act is currently just a draft in the Senate, and a long process of negotiation is needed before it becomes law. While the Trump administration is pushing for the bill's passage, the revenue restriction clause itself could become an obstacle. The revenue restriction may be revised or even removed entirely in the final text. The draft doesn't offer a blanket ban: rewards linked to payments, transfers, and promotional activities can still be offered, but passive income is no longer allowed. The SEC, CFTC, and Treasury will further define what constitutes "permitted rewards" within a year of the bill's enactment; the details are still pending. If Circle can transform its business model from "holding for rewards" to "using for rewards," the game might continue. Furthermore, USDC's growth isn't entirely driven by revenue. Polymarket, the world's largest prediction market, runs on USDC, and this transactional demand won't simply disappear because of a ban. Circle also launched Arc last year, a Layer-1 blockchain designed for stablecoin financial scenarios, covering global payments, foreign exchange, and asset tokenization, attempting to expand its business from stablecoin issuance to financial infrastructure. USDC won't die, but whether it can maintain the growth rate of the past year is a big question mark. Looking back, this crash served as a wake-up call for all crypto companies. In the past few years, the most successful companies in the crypto industry have all embraced regulation. Circle was a model student of this approach: proactive IPO, transparent audits, and active lobbying—all proving to Wall Street that it was a financial innovator in a suit. The market rewarded it; in less than a year since its IPO, its stock price has multiplied several times. Fourth-quarter revenue reached $770 million, a 76.9% year-over-year increase, with earnings per share of $0.43, significantly exceeding market expectations of $0.25. On paper, this is a rapidly growing, high-performing company. However, the CLARITY Act draft exposes an uncomfortable reality: compliance means voluntarily placing yourself within the regulatory range. Tether, operating overseas, is not directly affected by the ban, but Circle, as a US-listed company, can only obediently accept scrutiny. Compliance has given Circle armor, but also shackles. The bill is still under negotiation; the story is far from over. But this day in March 2026 is worth remembering: when innovation in the crypto world touches the boundaries of traditional finance, which way the scales tip in Capitol Hill ultimately depends on who has the bigger voice in Washington.

data.title: Web3 is sick, but the cure isn't AI.
data.description:
Author:Zhou, ChainCatcher

Over the past year,AI technology breakthroughs have far exceeded expectations.GPT-4oTo the emergence of variousAI Agentstools, new capabilities are being broken every few months.. The explosive popularity of next-generation AI Agent products, such as OpenClaw, has made many crypto professionals realize for the first time that AI is beginning to challenge the boundaries of their work. Thus, an anxiety surrounding "AI" has spread throughout the crypto community. From macro-strategies to micro-work methods, from leading institutions to frontline practitioners, many crypto projects and practitioners have begun to re-examine their own value and work models—will their work be replaced? Does the industry have a future? These questions have been repeatedly raised over the past year. I. Project Teams and Institutions Panic First [H2 style="text-align: left;">Product Responses

WhenAI Agentcan directly take over the keyboard and mouse, and perform tasks on the computer, users will be able to complete on-chain operations directly throughAI without needing to open exchanges or wallets. So, where will the value of exchanges' entry points be reflected?

Earlier this month, leading exchanges such as OKX, Binance, and Coinbase announced their AI product plans in quick succession.

Among them,OKX launched Onchain OS AI Upgrade, support AI Agent Across 60 Multiple blockchains and 500
